title: Orientation
html:
Shen Guo sent a message that evening. A name, Wei Rong, and an address, a tea house two subway stops from Jiang Wenxu's office, and a time: Saturday, 10 AM. Then a second message: she already knows about you. Don't be late.

He wrote it into the notebook and looked at the name. Wei Rong. No further context. He sent back: understood. What should I bring?

Shen Guo replied: your notebook, apparently.

He did not know what apparently meant and did not ask. He went to bed.

***

He was not late. He arrived eleven minutes early and spent the time examining the tea house from the outside, which was a habit from the instances that he had not yet recalibrated for ordinary situations. Ground floor of a residential building, the kind of corner unit that had been commercial for decades. The signage was small. The interior visible through the window was occupied by four people, none of them sitting alone.

At 9:58 he went in.

Wei Rong was at the corner table, back to the wall, facing the door. She was somewhere in her forties, fine-boned, wearing a grey coat that she had not taken off despite the warmth inside. She was reading something on her phone and did not look up when he came in, which he noted, she had positioned herself to see the door but was not performing attention.

He sat down across from her.

She put the phone down. She looked at him the way Shen Guo had in the hallway of the Bellhaven, the particular look of a recalibration, but faster, less visible, like the calculation completed before he had finished sitting.

"Jiang Wenxu," she said. Not a question.

"Yes."

"Two instances. Both cleared. No orientation, no prior knowledge, no game contact before the first pull." She folded her hands on the table. "You documented everything."

"It was the only available approach."

She looked at the jacket pocket where the notebook was. "May I?"

He considered for a moment. The notebook contained everything from both instances, his observations, the entity data, the exit mechanics, the notes left by prior players. It also contained his working assumptions about the game and his documentation of Chen Wei's situation. He took it out and put it on the table but did not push it toward her.

"When you're done, I have questions," he said.

The corner of her mouth moved. She opened the notebook and read.

He ordered tea. The kind he actually wanted, not the kind he usually ordered to get the interaction over with. He waited.

She read carefully. Not skimming, she went through each page at the pace of someone reading for structure rather than content, cross-referencing entries, occasionally going back a page. At the Ash Ward section she slowed. At the entity behavioral data she stopped and read a passage twice.

It took her fourteen minutes.

She closed the notebook and put it back on his side of the table.

"Your entity data on Ash Ward is more complete than anything currently in the game's shared record," she said. "No one has spent that long in the instance without dying. The ones who made it far enough to observe Entity B didn't make it out to document it."

"The pattern was consistent across five cycles before I moved. Consistent patterns are documentable."

"Most people don't wait five cycles."

"Waiting cost nothing. Moving before I had sufficient data would have cost the exit window."

She looked at him for a moment. Then she picked up her own tea. "What do you want to know?"

He had written the list on the train. He took out the notebook and opened it to the page. "Structure first. Then the Chen Wei problem. Then factions and administrators in that order."

"All right."

***

The game had been running, as far as anyone could determine, for somewhere between thirty and sixty years. The origin was disputed, there were three competing accounts among the major factions and no reliable external documentation. What was agreed on: it operated through the assignment of players to instances, the instances were graded by rank, and clearing an instance generated a performance record that accumulated under the assigned player's ID.

"Ranks," Jiang Wenxu said. "I've seen S and A. What's the full scale?"

"F through S, ascending. F-Rank is survivable by most players with basic awareness. S-Rank instances have a median survival rate under 3%. There's a theoretical tier above S that's been referenced in administrative records but never confirmed as an active rank."

He wrote: rank scale F through S, ascending difficulty. Median survival at S under 3%. Theoretical rank above S, unconfirmed. He looked up. "Ash Ward's survival probability was NULL. Below the median for S-Rank."

"Ash Ward is an outlier even within S-Rank. It's been active for eleven years. In that time, four players reached the second floor. None exited." She paused. "Until your run."

He noted this and moved on. "Assignment mechanism. How does the game select players?"

"That part isn't fully understood. The current working model is that the game identifies candidates based on criteria that have never been published, possible factors include psychological profile, physical condition, prior exposure to specific environments. Once identified, a player is assigned an ID and enters the queue." She looked at her cup. "The trigger for the first pull is typically a tagged object or location associated with the player's ID. In standard cases, the player receives orientation materials before the first instance."

"I didn't."

"You came in under Chen Wei's tag. The game assigned Chen Wei, sent orientation to Chen Wei, and then read your physical contact with his tag as the entry trigger. You skipped orientation because you aren't Chen Wei."

He had already worked most of this out. He confirmed the parts he had right and corrected the parts he hadn't. "The orientation materials. What do they cover?"

"Basic rank structure. Instance survival guidelines. Faction information. How to read system notifications. How to access the shared record, the observer channel and the game's historical instance data."

"I inferred most of it from the notification format."

"I know. I read your working assumptions." She set her cup down. "Your inference about the observer system is partially correct. The observer channel isn't just other players watching in real time. It's a structured archive, every player who clears an instance generates a viewable record that other players can access after the fact. The live observation component exists but it's a subset. What most players see is the post-instance record."

'So the Ash Ward record is archived and accessible. Everyone in the game can read what I did in there. The ANOMALOUS flag made it prominent.'

"How prominent?" he said.

Wei Rong was quiet for a moment. "The Ash Ward record has been accessed over two thousand times since your clear. The Bellhaven record crossed eight hundred within three hours of resolution." She looked at him steadily. "You are currently the most-watched active player in the game."

He wrote the numbers down. Two thousand. Eight hundred in three hours. He looked at what that meant practically: two thousand players had reviewed his methodology in Ash Ward and still did not know what they were looking at, if Park's comment about the observer channel was representative. His approach was documented but not legible to people reading it from outside.

'That is an asymmetry. They have the data and cannot use it. I have the process and can repeat it.'

"The Chen Wei problem," he said. "Shen Guo said the answer was complicated."

***

Wei Rong set her cup down and looked at the table for a moment before answering, which was the first time she had done that. Everything else had been direct.

"Under normal circumstances, when a player clears instances, the system accumulates performance data under their ID and eventually triggers a rank progression, the player moves from queue-entry level to a ranked tier, which changes what instances they can be assigned and at what frequency." She paused. "Two ANOMALOUS clears in a row is not normal. The system is almost certainly treating Chen Wei's record as a high-priority active player."

"Meaning it will pull him in."

"Meaning it already may have scheduled him. The queue position notification you described, instance queue position updated, estimated wait variable, that language is used for players who are past standard queue and into active scheduling. Variable wait means the system is looking for a trigger opportunity."

'It's looking for a trigger. The same way it found the umbrella tag. Something physically connected to Chen Wei, or a location he passes through.'

"He doesn't know any of this," Jiang Wenxu said.

"No."

"He got the orientation notification and dismissed it."

"Which means he has no framework for what happens when the pull comes. No instance knowledge, no behavioral data, no documentation." She looked at him. "What rank would he be assigned?"

"I don't know. The system has him clearing S and A-Rank instances. If rank progression works the way you described, it might assign him accordingly."

Wei Rong's expression did not change. But she did not correct him either, which was its own kind of answer.

'She's not saying I'm wrong. She's not confirming it. The rank progression question is one she doesn't want to answer directly.'

"That is a problem," he said.

"Yes."

"Is there a mechanism to correct the record. Reassign the clears to my ID instead of his."

"There are petition processes through the administrative tier. They're slow and the outcome isn't guaranteed. Administrators don't prioritize record corrections, they consider them low-urgency relative to active instance management."

"How slow?"

"Weeks. Months, if the case is complicated."

He wrote: record correction via administration, weeks to months, outcome not guaranteed, low priority classification. He looked at the timeline. If the system was already scheduling Chen Wei, weeks was too long. "Is there a faster path?"

"Faster paths exist but they involve faction resources." She paused. "Which brings us to your third question."

***

"There are four major factions," Wei Rong said. "The Clearance Bureau, the Archive, the Unaffiliated, and the Restructurers. Each has a different position on what the game is and what should be done about it."

"What are those positions?"

"The Clearance Bureau holds that the game is a fixed system and the correct response is to clear instances efficiently. They operate as a structured organization, ranks, resources, coordinated player teams. They're the largest faction by membership." She picked up her cup. "The Archive believes the game contains encoded information that can be extracted and used to understand its origin and purpose. They prioritize documentation and research over clearance rates."

He noted that the Archive's position aligned structurally with what he was already doing, without having been told about it. He did not comment on this.

"The Unaffiliated are players who don't join factions. They operate solo or in informal groups. Park would be one of those." He wrote it down. "The Restructurers believe the game can be altered or dismantled from within. They are the smallest faction and the most active in terms of interference with other players' runs."

"Interference meaning what?"

"Disruption. Deliberate introduction of bad data into the shared record. Occasionally, direct action inside instances."

'Direct action inside instances. Other players actively working against clearance. That is a threat category I haven't encountered yet.'

"You said faster paths involve faction resources," he said. "Which faction has the administrative access to expedite a record correction?"

"The Clearance Bureau has the closest relationships with the administrative tier. They've brokered corrections before."

"What would they want in exchange?"

Wei Rong looked at him with the same expression she had used when she read the Ash Ward entity data. "You," she said. "Specifically, affiliation. A player with your clearance record joining the Bureau would be significant for them."

"I'm not interested in joining a faction."

"I know. That's why this is complicated." She set her cup down again. "The Bureau will approach you regardless of whether you want affiliation. Your record makes you a resource they'll want to secure. The question is whether you engage on your terms or theirs."

"What are my terms."

"That depends on what you want."

He looked at his notebook. What he wanted was straightforward: Chen Wei protected, the record corrected, enough information to survive the next instance, and sufficient time to build out his documentation before the system assigned him something he didn't have data for. None of those required faction membership. All of them were complicated by the fact that other people in the game had agendas he couldn't yet map.

"The Bureau," he said. "Do they approach directly or through intermediaries?"

"Both. Depending on how much they want the player."

"Have they already moved on my record?"

Wei Rong considered the question. "A Bureau representative accessed your Ash Ward record six hours after the clear. Your Bellhaven record within forty minutes of resolution." She paused. "They're fast when they're interested."

"So I should expect contact."

"I would expect it has already been arranged."

He wrote: Bureau, record access confirmed, Ash Ward 6 hours post-clear, Bellhaven 40 minutes post-resolution, contact likely incoming or already scheduled. He looked at what he had. Factions, administrators, a record accumulating under the wrong name, Chen Wei in an active scheduling queue with no context.

'The scope of what I don't know has decreased. The scope of what I now need to manage has increased. These are not the same thing.'

"One more question," he said. "You do orientations for players who came in wrong. How many players have you oriented?"

"Eleven, over six years."

"How many of them cleared their first instance before coming to you?"

Wei Rong looked at him. "None," she said. "Usually they come to me because they survived and don't know how they did it. They need the framework to understand what happened."

"And I came to you after two clears."

"With a notebook."

He accepted that. "What do I owe you?"

"Forward me your instance documentation after each clear. What you write in that notebook, the entity data, the environmental mapping, the exit mechanics. There are instances in the game's record that have no reliable data because no one who went in came out. Your documentation fills gaps."

"You're Archive," he said.

She did not confirm or deny it. She picked up the check.

***

He left the tea house at 11:43. The street outside was busy in the way Saturday streets were, people moving without particular urgency, a market stall two blocks down doing steady business. He stood at the corner for a moment and added a final entry to the notebook.

Orientation complete. Key updates: rank scale confirmed F through S, above-S unconfirmed. Chen Wei in active scheduling, trigger-dependent, timeframe unknown. Record correction requires faction resources or administrative petition, weeks to months. Bureau contact incoming. Wei Rong, Archive affiliation, probable, information exchange agreed, documentation forwarded post-clear.

He looked at the Chen Wei line.

Trigger-dependent. The system was looking for a physical contact point, the same way the umbrella tag had been a contact point. Which meant Chen Wei needed to avoid triggering it, and Chen Wei didn't know what a trigger looked like.

He needed to tell Chen Wei something. Not everything, he still didn't have enough data to make everything useful, but enough that Chen Wei could avoid the obvious entry points. Enough that if a notification came through, Chen Wei would not dismiss it as spam.

'This requires a conversation I don't have sufficient information to have correctly. But insufficient information is not the same as no information. What I have is enough to reduce his risk. That is the current objective.'

He put the notebook away and walked to the subway.

On the train he drafted what he was going to say to Chen Wei. He wrote three versions, crossed out two, and arrived at his stop with one that was accurate, reasonably complete given the gaps, and would probably produce more questions than it answered. He could work with that. Questions meant Chen Wei was processing rather than dismissing.

His phone buzzed.

The message was from a number he didn't have saved. It read: We've been watching your record. When you have time, we should talk., Bureau of Clearance, Outreach Division.

He looked at it for a moment. Then he saved the number under: Bureau, do not engage without preparation. He wrote the message text and the timestamp into the notebook.

Wei Rong had said the contact was likely already arranged. The timing suggested she had known more specifically than that.

He filed it and kept walking.
